summary: DeVault announces visurf, a new web browser frontend for the NetSurf engine with vi-inspired keybindings and a lightweight Wayland UI. He positions it as a deliberate rejection of the modern web's complexity, preferring NetSurf's limited HTML4/CSS2 support over the bloated codebases of Firefox and Chromium-based browsers. The project aims to create a usable browser, drive interest in NetSurf development, and encourage websites to build for scope-constrained browsers. He also announces plans to overhaul SourceHut's frontend with a new CSS framework that treats small browsers as first-class targets.
key_points: NetSurf is a small, independent browser engine supporting mostly HTML4/CSS2, deliberately limited compared to WebKit and Gecko
DeVault abandoned qutebrowser for Firefox due to stability issues, but finds Firefox equally problematic as a monstrous codebase owned by an increasingly indifferent organization
The project has three goals: build a nice browser, drive NetSurf development, and encourage websites to support scope-constrained browsers
SourceHut's frontend will be overhauled with a new HTML+CSS framework treating small browsers like NetSurf as first-class targets
Planned features include readability mode, automatic redirects to privacy-respecting frontends like Nitter, and potentially Gemini protocol support
money_quotes: Given the epidemic of complexity in the modern web, I am pleased by the idea of a small browser, more limited in scope, which perhaps requires the cooperation of like-minded websites to support a pleasant experience.
But I reject the modern web, and qute is just as much a behemoth of complexity as the rest of its lot.

Firefox has more problems besides — it may be somewhat more stable, but it's ultimately still an obscenely complex, monsterous codebase, owned by an organization which cares less and less about my needs with each passing day.
A new solution is called for.
I don't want to see it become a "modern" web browser, and frankly I think that's not even possible, but I would be pleased to see more people helping to improve its existing features.

The goal for this effort will be to provide a framework that allows for conservative use of newer browser features, with suitable fallbacks, with enough room for each website to express its own personality in a manner which is beautiful and useful on all manner of web browsers.
key_insight: Rather than accepting the modern web's complexity as inevitable, DeVault proposes building both a browser and a web framework ecosystem around deliberate simplicity, requiring cooperation between browser and website developers.
